schools.summary: Bad teachers do not nullify good doctrine; the office (Moses' seat) is honoured though the man is guilty — take the gold, leave the dross.
Henry: Christ allows their office but condemns the men; good offices are not abolished because bad men abuse them.
Calvin: the word's authority must remain entire amid the scribes' vices; Christ vindicates the Law before exposing its corrupt ministers.
Ellicott: Christ closes his public teaching as he began, protesting against the traditions that supplant God's commandments.
'Moses' seat' = authority to teach and expound the Law (the Sanhedrin / the chair of interpretation); they sit now by divine permission.
Calvin: obey only so far as they expound the Law purely; the verse gives no warrant for binding obedience to prelates' inventions (Matthew 16:6).
'All' is qualified to sound exposition of the Law (Christ elsewhere condemns their traditions); follow their teaching, never their example.
Clarke: the command cannot be absolute, since Christ warns against their false teaching; perhaps refers to a purer former teaching.
The Pharisees are taskmasters, exacting the Law's heavy rites (Acts 15:10) from others while not lifting a finger themselves.
Chrysostom: a double evil — utmost rigour to others, large licence to self; a good ruler is severe to himself, merciful to others.
Calvin: not unjust laws but a stern, rigid teaching that demands of others what one will not do oneself (Ezekiel 34:4).
Antiquarian detail on phylacteries (tephillin) and fringes (Numbers 15:38); enlarged for show, taken as charms — zeal displacing obedience.
Vainglory is the corrupting worm; Jerome: the precepts were to be worn on the heart, not the body — the Pharisees misread the literal sign.
Calvin: their seeming-good is worthless, aimed only to please men; God's aids to remembrance turned to display.
The chief couches at feasts and front seats by the ark in synagogues, coveted as marks of religious rank — a timeless hunger for prominence.
Calvin: the love of precedence proves ambition, not piety — preferring human honour to God's approval.
Public greetings and the title 'Rabbi' (a comparatively new honour, from Gamaliel's time) craved as marks of religious eminence — the public face of ambition.
Disciples are not to seek the title 'Rabbi' — all are brethren under one Teacher; Barnes applies it against honorific titles that engender pride.
Wesley: the title implied implicit belief and obedience owed to God alone; forbidden to give or receive such reverence.
Not natural or civil 'father' but spiritual authority over conscience is forbidden; Ellicott traces the parallel abuse in Pope, Abbot, 'Father in God.'
Calvin: the honour of father is falsely given to a man when it obscures God's glory; yet Paul is a spiritual father (1 Corinthians 4:15).
'Masters' = guides/leaders who direct the conscience; forbidden as empty marks of superiority, not civil titles; Christ alone is the sole teacher.
Calvin: the repetition seals that God alone rules and Christ is the only head of the Church (Ephesians 1:22).
Greatness shown in ministration, not in asserting rank (Ellicott); service is the way to true honour (cf. Matthew 20:26).
Calvin: the highest honour in the Church is service, not government; whatever the title, the servant takes nothing from God or Christ.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here is misplaced material on the labourers parable, not this verse.

schools.summary: A settled law of God, often repeated (Luke 14:11; 18:14; James 4:6): self-exaltation abased, humility exalted.
Clarke: God humbled himself to death to bring pride into contempt; Wesley notes the saying recurs at least ten times.
Henry: eight woes against the eight beatitudes; the first — sworn enemies of the gospel who neither enter nor let others enter (John 7:48).
Origen: the woe, like the Law's curse, falls from the sinner's own deeds; hindering others itself shuts out the hinderer.
Calvin: the vehemence aims to withdraw the simple from a deadly sect, as one must thunder against corrupt clergy.
The 'hypocrites' formula begins the woes; v.14 is absent from the best manuscripts.
Chrysostom: plundering widows' poverty under cover of long prayer earns 'greater damnation' — godliness made a cloak for wickedness.
They became guardians/heirs of widows' estates while feigning sanctity by hours-long prayer; the verse is absent from many MSS (cf. Mark 12:40).
Calvin: they sold prayers like hirelings for gain, profaning God's name the more fervent the show.
The convert is damned by imitating the masters' deeds, 'twofold' because he chose this path; the motive was gain or vainglory, not compassion.
Historical zeal for proselytes (Hyrcanus' forced conversions); 'leprosy of Israel'; the more converted the more perverted (Robertson).
Calvin: alluring Gentiles to a degenerate Judaism hurried them from Scylla to Charybdis, as the monks make devils of their converts.
The oath-casuistry served covetousness (gold binds, temple does not); swearing by the creature swears by the God who rules it (Gloss).
Ellicott (cf. Matthew 5:33-37): the arbitrary degrees of sanctity collapse — every oath runs up into the thought of God; 'blind guides.'
Calvin: covetous pastors exalted gold and gift as a scheme for prey, undervaluing the majesty of God and temple.
The consecrating temple is greater than the gold it sanctifies; their inversion betrayed avarice. Robertson: 'fools' here is the Judge's just verdict, not the anger forbidden in Matthew 5:22.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to the labourers parable, not this verse.

schools.summary: The same casuistry applied to altar and gift; the brazen altar of burnt-offering (2 Chronicles 4:1) and the gift devoted to God.
Calvin: Christ leads to the source — the altar (displaying God's glory) is greater than the gift; every oath must honour God's name alone.
A near-verbatim repetition for altar/gift: the consecrating altar is greater than the gift it sanctifies (Exodus 29:37); the graded-oath system is absurd and corrupt.
An oath by the altar invokes all upon it; God witnesses every form, so the 'exempt' cases are illusory (Clarke).
Wesley: every oath by a creature is an implicit appeal to God; Christ does not endorse such swearing but closes the evasion (cf. Matthew 5:33-37).

themes: swear by the altar and all on it

schools.summary: To swear by the temple is to swear by God who dwelt there (the Shekinah); Clarke notes the past-tense reading 'dwelt' hints God had already forsaken it (cf. v.38).

outliers.note: Presses the variant 'dwelt' (past tense) as hinting the divine presence had departed the temple, anticipating v.38.

schools.summary: The climax: to swear by heaven is to swear by God's throne and God himself (Matthew 5:34); every real oath calls God to witness, so the graded evasions fail.
Scrupulous tithing of valueless herbs while omitting judgment, mercy, faith; Christ blames not the tithing but the substitution of lesser for greater.
They swallow the great commands and fuss over profitable trifles; the herbs are seasoning, not substance (Origen) — yet 'these ought ye to have done.'
Calvin: Christ values the whole Law (Galatians 3:10) but enforces due order — the commandments are interwoven, not to be detached.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to the labourers parable, not this verse.

schools.summary: The proverb is 'strain OUT a gnat' (a 1611 misprint gives 'at'); both gnat and camel were unclean (Leviticus 11) — meticulous in trifles, reckless in great sins.
Calvin: hypocrites shrink from tiny faults yet swallow heinous crimes, rigid in the negligible while tearing the whole Law.
The cup figures the man — outward washing avails nothing while the inside is foul; Chrysostom: external purity must follow internal.
The cup is filled with the proceeds of extortion (harpages) and graft (akrasias) — wickedness in high places, clean without, foul within.
Calvin: 'the outside' is the appearance regulated only before men's eyes; Wesley: unjustly procured, intemperately used.
The remedy: cleanse the inside first (the heart, the source) and the outside follows; moral, not surface, purity (cf. Matthew 15:18-20).
Tombs whitewashed each Adar to warn of defilement (Numbers 19:16), beautiful without, full of bones within — distinct from the unmarked graves of Luke 11:44.
Feigned righteousness is dead, like an actor's role; 'either be what you seem, or seem what you are' (Pseudo-Chrysostom).
Calvin: hypocrites are marble tombs over a carcass — God's children should desire to be pure, not to appear so.
The application: outwardly righteous, inwardly full of 'lawlessness'; their own rabbis condemned the 'painted' Pharisee (works of Zimri, reward of Phinehas).
Clarke: a fair reputation will not screen the heart at the judgment — 'see that thy heart be right with God.'
Building prophets' tombs is a subtle self-witness of murderers' sons; the design is a triumphal monument, not honour (Chrysostom).
The Herodian monuments by Olivet, adorned even as they plot the death of a greater Prophet; 'their words are their monuments' (Talmud).
Calvin: hypocrites honour dead teachers whose reproofs no longer annoy — 'if Christ were here, they would burn him.'
The disclaimer is unconscious hypocrisy — every generation condemns the past's sins then commits them; the deceit may be sincere, which damns the more (Ellicott).
Calvin: their veneration of dead prophets is no expiation; boasting of sacred ancestry, they imitate the fathers by murdering the living.
Their own words convict them: 'unto yourselves' — children of the prophet-killers not only by descent but by an approving spirit (Acts 7:52).
Clarke/Wesley: those who reject the gospel now would have slain Christ then; Wesley summarizes the whole woe-section against false religion.
Chrysostom: not a command but a prophecy — 'do what you are about to do'; they exceed the fathers, crucifying God, drawing accumulated judgment.
An ironical imperative; the nation fills up its iniquity by slaying the Messiah, exhausting God's patience.
Calvin: their veneration of the dead is no expiation; they imitate the fathers in murdering the living.
The climax echoes the Baptist (Matthew 3:7) and Matthew 12:34; serpents/vipers = inward poison and hypocrisy; Barnes cautions ministers not to imitate language only Christ could justly use.
Calvin: vehement indignation shakes off the hypocrites' flatteries and strikes terror into the people to guard against like destruction.
Henry: Christ yet tries the doomed generation with the means of grace, sending Christian prophets and wise men — a final overture that fills up their measure.
Fulfilled in the persecution of the Christian ministry — Stephen, James (Acts 7:60; 12:2; 1 Thessalonians 2:15); 'I send' is the word of the Lord of the prophets.
Calvin: Christ speaks as God, the Eternal Wisdom who presided over the Church and sent the prophets from the beginning.

Zacharias = the son of Jehoiada (2 Chronicles 24:20-22), last martyr in the Hebrew canon's order; 'son of Barachias' explained by double names, a Nazarenes reading, or a scribal note; temporal judgment descends on the nation.
Calvin: shared guilt of the whole conspiring nation; Abel (with Cain as head) the first and Zechariah a chosen ancient martyr, not the latest.

debates.question: Who is Zacharias son of Barachias?

sides.position: Zechariah son of Jehoiada (2 Chronicles 24), the name varying by double-name or scribal note
Possibly the Zechariah son of Baruch slain by Zealots (Josephus), by prophetic foresight

schools.summary: 'This generation' = the contemporaries, who within forty years saw Jerusalem fall; accumulated guilt becomes righteous judgment when a generation makes the fathers' sins its own (Ellicott).

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here is a catechetical aside on the Holy Spirit, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the doubled name marks compassion and intense love; their murders had not alienated him — he longed to gather them many times.
A grieved love (cf. Luke 13:34; 19:41); 'ye would not' states man's fatal freedom to frustrate even God's pity (Ellicott).
Calvin: indignation toward the scribes, pity for the city; the revealed will (the word calling all) is in view, not the secret counsel — accommodated to our weakness.
Augustine: the mystery of the will — yet he gathered those children whom he would.

debates.question: How does 'I would... ye would not' bear on God's will and human freedom?

sides.position: It expresses man's free power to frustrate God's love and pity
It is God's revealed will (the universal call), distinct from his secret counsel

schools.summary: 'Your house' (not God's) — the temple forsaken as Christ departs it (Matthew 24:1), doomed to Roman destruction; 'desolate' omitted in some MSS.
Calvin: the sentence shatters their false confidence in the temple as an invincible fortress; God, driven away by their crimes, leaves it no longer his.
Abandonment now, but a far-off promise: Israel will at last say 'Blessed is he that cometh' (Psalms 118:26), pointing to a future conversion / second Advent (Romans 11:25-32).
Clarke: till the fulness of the Gentiles is brought in; Christ judges as lawgiver yet weeps as a father — not a necessitating decree of reprobation.
